author: @davidvwilliamson
text: i don't know how much stretch there is in that copper wire; the crushing force exerted by one turn on the reel is trivial but by the finish it will be multiplied by 1000. A curious fact i stumbled upon because of my kite flying. Plastic reels of fishing line (and eventually purpose-made wooden ones) would inexplicably go a funny shape because each turn added an additional crushing force. Took a long time for the penny to drop.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @GavinImpett the big cog at the back of the counter need to engage with a cog the same size on your coil wheel (at the centre of the coil wheel - you need to connect these cogs either with an idle gear or a chain

author: @charlesjmouse
text:
Always excellent and interesting. Thoughts based on inexperience so feel entirely free to ignore me. Other ways to skin a cat:
I presume wire is supplied by the length. Could one vastly simplify the coil winding machine by picking an appropriate length of wire for size of coil and number of turns one wishes to make and simply use all the wire?
I guess such a scheme may not be fantastically accurate for a number of reasons and you would be limited by the lengths supplied, but potentially good enough? 

Another option:
If one powered a coil winding machine with a stepper motor it should be possible to work out very accurately how many steps per revolution. A stepper driver with enough 'intelligence' to count the steps, set it to the desired number of turns and come back when finished. I imagine a 3D printer driver board would be suitable if something simpler isn't readily available. (An ordinary motor plus a Hall sensor on the winder with an electronic counter to shut off the motor might suffice)
An automated winder might be be 'better' and without a mechanical counter may tolerate much higher winding speeds. However is watching one's own mechanical contrivance doing it's thing more satisfying?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Your wire suggestion won't really be that useful mate as people make all kinds of coil sizes and shapes and wire is supplied by weight - you can calculate everything - of course - but it turns out it is much much simpler just to wind it when you need it - for example the coil of wire i have for the fine wire is about 20,000km long - and i am not kidding - As for automation - sure that can be done easily enough - but i like mechanics
